Effective planning of human resources is critical in designing an efficient home healthcare system. In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts: Nickel et al. ( 2012 ) proposed a fairly comprehensive HHC planning model that considered nurses’ availability, working hours, qualifications patient time windows and visit frequency. The model was solved with a two-stage approach where a feasible solution is provided by a constraint programming heuristic in the first stage and improved by adaptive large neighbourhood search in the second stage. A model to construct a good master schedule that centred on a high degree of continuity was also proposed. Real-world data for up to 7 days, 12 nurses and 95 visits were solved within 2 hours.

excerpts: Effective planning of human resources is critical in designing an efficient home healthcare system. In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

In this article, we investigate a multi-day multi-shift home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem. Decisions made for each period consequently impact decisions for subsequent periods. Each patient requires a given number of visits per day, with specific requirements for each visit. All visits must be conducted while adhering to their requirements as hard constraints. Additionally, each caregiver has a certain qualification level and is pre-scheduled to work specific shifts during the planning horizon.

We address three objectives: minimizing the number of extra shifts, maximizing the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimizing the sum of route durations. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of constraints, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts: Laps Care , a system that was developed in 2002, uses operations research modeling to eliminate the manual planning of home care unit assignments. More than 200 units/organizations in Swedish municipalities use Laps Care each day to plan staff scheduling and routing for 4,000 home care workers. The system has increased operational efficiency by 10–15 percent; this corresponds to an annual savings of 20–30 million euros ($30–$45 million).

The City of Stockholm, with its 800,000 inhabitants, adopted Laps Care in 2006 and started a full implementation and rollout during 2008, thus adding 800 units and 15,000 home care workers to the system. The savings for the City of Stockholm will be 20–30 million euros ($30–$45 million).

In a home care setting, high-quality care is typically associated with continuity of care. In addition, the increasing pressure due to labor shortages calls for cost-efficient operations. This paper focuses on obtaining cost-efficient daily schedules over a longer time horizon, with balanced shift lengths, while ensuring continuity of care (using the continuity of care index). To address this challenge, we propose a novel method based on blueprint routes. This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages. To ensure continuity of care, the daily scheduling decisions are strategically guided using the concept named blueprint routes. The blueprint routes are pre-optimized (partial) routes that help to align the daily schedules to achieve continuity of care in the subsequent nurse-to-shift assignment. Model-based evolutionary algorithms are employed to overcome the NP-hardness of the routing problem and nurse-to-shift assignment. Real-life-based numerical experiments demonstrate that continuity of care does not have to compromise home care schedule costs significantly.

assesses continuity of care in relation to home care scheduling, using the Continuity of Care Index (CCI), as defined in, e.g., [[7]]() , [[14]]() and used in, e.g., [[11]]() . The CCI is designed to mitigate potential issues associated with other measurement methods. It incorporates factors such as the frequency of visits, the range of nurses involved, and the care coordination among nurses

The blueprint routes, that are pre-optimized upfront, comprise sets of (partial) efficient routes via jobs with frequent occurrences throughout the time horizon. These blueprint routes are designed to serve as guiding structures during the daily scheduling optimization, laying the foundation to facilitate continuity of care in the nurse assignment over the complete time horizon.

Additionally, each caregiver has a certain qualification level and is pre-scheduled to work specific shifts during the planning horizon.

We address three objectives: minimizing the number of extra shifts, maximizing the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimizing the sum of route durations. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of constraints, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts: In a home care setting, high-quality care is typically associated with continuity of care. In addition, the increasing pressure due to labor shortages calls for cost-efficient operations. This paper focuses on obtaining cost-efficient daily schedules over a longer time horizon, with balanced shift lengths, while ensuring continuity of care (using the continuity of care index). To address this challenge, we propose a novel method based on blueprint routes. This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages. To ensure continuity of care, the daily scheduling decisions are strategically guided using the concept named blueprint routes. The blueprint routes are pre-optimized (partial) routes that help to align the daily schedules to achieve continuity of care in the subsequent nurse-to-shift assignment. Model-based evolutionary algorithms are employed to overcome the NP-hardness of the routing problem and nurse-to-shift assignment. Real-life-based numerical experiments demonstrate that continuity of care does not have to compromise home care schedule costs significantly.

In [[20]]() the HHCRSP is solved using a branch-and-price algorithm. This algorithm is divided into two main components: a pricing problem that generates daily schedules for individual nurses and a master problem that combines these daily schedules into a feasible overall solution. The objective of their model consists of travel time, nurse busyness, and quality of service, which includes continuity of care.
The authors in [[13]]() combine travel time with continuity of care and preference costs between nurses and clients.

When it comes to the nurse assignment, continuity of care is often studied together with the workload balance of the nurses. In many of these studies, the main focus is to optimize the workload balance, while continuity of care is of secondary importance by including it as a constraint (e.g., by limiting the number of unique nurses that visit a client), see for example [[32]]() , [[33]]() , [[34]]() .

In terms of rostering, our approach parallels that of [[38]]() , where we primarily focus on continuity of care rather than an even distribution of workload among nurses. Albeit we do not specifically balance the workload, our shift design and penalty for overtime implicitly allow for a balanced workload distribution.

To solve the set partitioning problem, the authors employ a large neighborhood search method to generate potential routes, which are then assigned to nurses using a constructive set partitioning heuristic.

excerpts: The HHCRSP model is built to minimize the total operating cost and maximize the work balance of the caregiver, which increases the complexity of the problem.

To sum up, most HHCRSP studies usually consider the goal of minimizing cost or distance, ignoring the workload balance and skill matching problems in the actual service process, which results in a large deviation from the actual situation.
The existing research distinguishes the home health care service and home delivery service by using the skill level of the caregiver, which simplifies the combination of problems to a certain extent.

Therefore, this paper introduces the home delivery route separately, and considers the skills matching problem and the home delivery problem on the basis of the traditional home health care scheduling problem.

excerpts: Effective planning of human resources is critical in designing an efficient home healthcare system. In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

In this article, we investigate a multi-day multi-shift home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem. Decisions made for each period consequently impact decisions for subsequent periods. Each patient requires a given number of visits per day, with specific requirements for each visit. All visits must be conducted while adhering to their requirements as hard constraints. Additionally, each caregiver has a certain qualification level and is pre-scheduled to work specific shifts during the planning horizon.

We address three objectives: minimizing the number of extra shifts, maximizing the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimizing the sum of route durations. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of constraints, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts:
**3 Problem Description and Model.**
3.1 Consistent Home Health Care Scheduling Problem
(Con-HHCSP).
Home Health Care Scheduling Problem (HHCSP) is a
variant of a multi-depot, multi-vehicle routing problem
with labor-related and time-related constraints. We be-
gin by describing the problem. Each day, each HHA
is assigned a set of patients to be visited at scheduled
times. The HHA travels from his home to deliver care
to his assigned patients in accordance with the assigned
schedule. The HHA travels from patient to patient; fol-
lowing completion of his daily assignments, the HHA
returns home. We consider deterministic travel times
and distances between patient locations; these distances
may be calculated using a geographic information sys-
tem tool. Service times are also considered to be deter-
ministic, having a value roughly estimated based on
prior experience. In our problem setting, we assume
common service times across all patients.

Time-related constraints enforce regular working hours,
 ... 
or more HHAs that they prefer and/or characteristics
of HHAs that they prefer (e.g., gender, languages spo-
ken). These preferences are treated as soft constraints
where a penalty is introduced to the objective function
for each preference violation.
Similar to the labor-related constraints, having soft
constraints for the assignment decisions is not common
in the literature. In most cases, staff assignments are
restricted due to hard constraints such as skills require-
ments. Modeling preferences as a soft constraint and in-
troducing a penalty for preference violation introduces
the challenge of appropriately selecting a penalty.

The consistent home health care scheduling prob-
lem (Con-HHCSP) arises when there is a need to solve
HHCSP to create a new schedule due to a change in

patients requiring care) and there is a desire to retain

excerpts: The HHCRSP model is built to minimize the total operating cost and maximize the work balance of the caregiver, which increases the complexity of the problem.

To sum up, most HHCRSP studies usually consider the goal of minimizing cost or distance, ignoring the workload balance and skill matching problems in the actual service process, which results in a large deviation from the actual situation.
The existing research distinguishes the home health care service and home delivery service by using the skill level of the caregiver, which simplifies the combination of problems to a certain extent.

Therefore, this paper introduces the home delivery route separately, and considers the skills matching problem and the home delivery problem on the basis of the traditional home health care scheduling problem.

excerpts: Laps Care , a system that was developed in 2002, uses operations research modeling to eliminate the manual planning of home care unit assignments. More than 200 units/organizations in Swedish municipalities use Laps Care each day to plan staff scheduling and routing for 4,000 home care workers. The system has increased operational efficiency by 10–15 percent; this corresponds to an annual savings of 20–30 million euros ($30–$45 million).

The City of Stockholm, with its 800,000 inhabitants, adopted Laps Care in 2006 and started a full implementation and rollout during 2008, thus adding 800 units and 15,000 home care workers to the system. The savings for the City of Stockholm will be 20–30 million euros ($30–$45 million).

basis.reasoning: The most relevant material directly addresses continuity of care in home health care scheduling, including explicit attention to continuity scores, the Continuity of Care Index (CCI), and mechanisms to preserve CoC over time. For instance, works that measure and optimize continuity alongside routing costs describe using CoC metrics (such as continuity scores and the CCI) and hard constraints or penalty structures to maintain continuity, which aligns with the field value describing CoC as a critical objective and discussing how to measure it (e.g., number of unique caregivers or CCI). Excerpts describing novel constructs like blueprint routes, which predefine recurring patterns to guide scheduling toward higher continuity while controlling costs, provide concrete modeling techniques that implement CoC objectives in practice. Excerpts that discuss rolling-horizon formulations with inconsistency variables illustrate how to penalize caregivers who do not re-visit the same patient across periods, tying directly to continuity-oriented objective functions. Additional excerpts describe set partitioning formulations and other optimization frameworks that incorporate CoC considerations, showing how CoC can be integrated into broader multi-objective or exact methods (e.g., branch-and-price, SPP). Collectively, these sources form a cohesive view of CoC as an objective and as a set of modeling tools (metrics, penalties, hard constraints, blueprint routes) used in home care scheduling, which matches the target fine-grained field value. The remaining excerpts supplement this by providing contextual background on CoC measurement, comparisons of CoC with workload balance, and surveys of methods that touch on CoC but with broader scope, thus offering supporting context without directly detailing CoC mechanics as strongly as the primary set.

excerpts: The costs associated to the routes include travel time, continuity of care scores, shift length violations, and the number of unmet client preferences.

In [[20]]() the HHCRSP is solved using a branch-and-price algorithm. This algorithm is divided into two main components: a pricing problem that generates daily schedules for individual nurses and a master problem that combines these daily schedules into a feasible overall solution. The objective of their model consists of travel time, nurse busyness, and quality of service, which includes continuity of care.

When it comes to the nurse assignment, continuity of care is often studied together with the workload balance of the nurses. In many of these studies, the main focus is to optimize the workload balance, while continuity of care is of secondary importance by including it as a constraint (e.g., by limiting the number of unique nurses that visit a client), see for example [[32]]() , [[33]]() , [[34]]() .

In terms of rostering, our approach parallels that of [[38]]() , where we primarily focus on continuity of care rather than an even distribution of workload among nurses. Albeit we do not specifically balance the workload, our shift design and penalty for overtime implicitly allow for a balanced workload distribution.

To solve the set partitioning problem, the authors employ a large neighborhood search method to generate potential routes, which are then assigned to nurses using a constructive set partitioning heuristic.

In a home care setting, high-quality care is typically associated with continuity of care. In addition, the increasing pressure due to labor shortages calls for cost-efficient operations. This paper focuses on obtaining cost-efficient daily schedules over a longer time horizon, with balanced shift lengths, while ensuring continuity of care (using the continuity of care index). To address this challenge, we propose a novel method based on blueprint routes. This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages. To ensure continuity of care, the daily scheduling decisions are strategically guided using the concept named blueprint routes. The blueprint routes are pre-optimized (partial) routes that help to align the daily schedules to achieve continuity of care in the subsequent nurse-to-shift assignment. Model-based evolutionary algorithms are employed to overcome the NP-hardness of the routing problem and nurse-to-shift assignment. Real-life-based numerical experiments demonstrate that continuity of care does not have to compromise home care schedule costs significantly.

assesses continuity of care in relation to home care scheduling, using the Continuity of Care Index (CCI), as defined in, e.g., [[7]]() , [[14]]() and used in, e.g., [[11]]() . The CCI is designed to mitigate potential issues associated with other measurement methods. It incorporates factors such as the frequency of visits, the range of nurses involved, and the care coordination among nurses

The blueprint routes, that are pre-optimized upfront, comprise sets of (partial) efficient routes via jobs with frequent occurrences throughout the time horizon. These blueprint routes are designed to serve as guiding structures during the daily scheduling optimization, laying the foundation to facilitate continuity of care in the nurse assignment over the complete time horizon.

basis.reasoning: The trend toward balancing continuity of care with route costs and the use of blueprint routes is repeatedly described as a central approach to structuring daily schedules and guiding nurse-to-shift assignments over longer horizons. In particular, blueprint routes are presented as pre-optimized partial routes that guide daily schedules to achieve continuity of care in subsequent nurse assignments, while explicitly accounting for travel time, time windows, and shift costs. These passages illustrate a shift from ad-hoc or purely cost-driven planning to structured, continuity-aware planning that integrates routing and scheduling decisions across days. The discussion of blueprint routes being used to align daily schedules and improve continuity in the full horizon embodies the move toward Integrated Planning and Continuity-First design that the field is embracing. Moreover, the literature describes solving the HHCRSP with a branch-and-price framework, where the master problem handles scheduling and rostering and the pricing subproblem handles route construction, underscoring the push toward advanced decomposition techniques rather than simple heuristics. This aligns with the identified evolution toward Exact and Matheuristic methods (e.g., CP for initial feasible solutions followed by metaheuristics like ALNS) and the use of sophisticated decomposition (branch-and-price, LBBD) to tackle large, integrated problems. Additional excerpts emphasize the broader research shift to stochastic and robust optimization, where models incorporate uncertain travel and service times via stochastic programming with recourse, sample averaging, and robust formulations, often embedded within LBBD or branch-and-price frameworks. This demonstrates a clear movement away from deterministic, static models toward probabilistic, resilient optimization. The literature also highlights dynamic and online scheduling, including rolling-horizon approaches that re-optimize as new information arrives, which is consistent with a trend toward solutions that adapt over time rather than static plans. Finally, there is a consistent emphasis on integrated planning that jointly considers staff rostering, shift scheduling, and daily visit routing to balance cost, continuity, and fairness over longer horizons, illustrating a holistic move toward more capable and realistic models. Taken together, these excerpts corroborate a coherent narrative: research in home care optimization is advancing from simple heuristics to integrated, decomposed, and probabilistic models that explicitly address continuity of care, workforce constraints, and uncertainty, while leveraging modern solution frameworks like LBBD, branch-and-price, CP, ALNS, and blueprint-route concepts.

The second strongest support comes from passages that name and describe Column Generation and Branch-and-Price as standard exact methods for solving large-set-partitioning formulations of HHCRSP, including the pricing subproblem structure and the integration with master problems. Additional entries that explicitly list the presence and role of these techniques within HHCRSP contexts reinforce the relevance, showing how LBBD, CG, and B&P have been used or proposed in related works. Taken together, these excerpts directly substantiate the fine-grained field value components and their described roles in HHCRSP solution methods.

basis.reasoning: The field value centers on four objective families that are routinely modeled in home health care routing and scheduling: (a) economic efficiency, (b) continuity of care, (c) fairness in workload, and (d) accommodation of preferences. The excerpts provide direct evidence for each of these families. For economic costs, multiple excerpts describe minimizing travel time/distance and staff-related costs as core goals, often within a bi- or multi-objective framework and using optimization methods that balance time windows and routing efficiency. For continuity of care, several excerpts discuss continuity-focused formulations, metrics like continuity indices, and strategies to limit caregiver turnover or to align assignments across periods to sustain care consistency. For workload fairness, the literature frequently introduces balance considerations and min-max or max-min style objectives, sometimes pairing them with continuity considerations in weekly or multi-period planning. For preferences, soft constraints and penalties for not meeting caregiver-patient pairings or preferences appear in discussions about satisfaction and personalized care delivery. Several excerpts explicitly combine these objectives in a single problem setting, often employing set partitioning, constraint programming, large neighborhood search, or logic-based decompositions to handle the interplay among time windows, skill matching, synchronization, continuity, and workload. The content of these excerpts also highlights the common methodological toolbox (set partitioning, CP/ALNS, LBBD, DAM-BPC, branch-and-price) used to operationalize these objectives, illustrating how the stated objectives are translated into tractable models and solution approaches. Overall, the excerpts collectively validate the multi-objective view and the specific objective categories claimed in the field value, demonstrating that home care routing and scheduling is typically modeled as a multi-objective optimization problem with explicit emphasis on cost, continuity of care, fairness, and preferences, implemented through various exact and heuristic solution methods.

excerpts: Effective planning of human resources is critical in designing an efficient home healthcare system. In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts: The costs associated to the routes include travel time, continuity of care scores, shift length violations, and the number of unmet client preferences.

In [[20]]() the HHCRSP is solved using a branch-and-price algorithm. This algorithm is divided into two main components: a pricing problem that generates daily schedules for individual nurses and a master problem that combines these daily schedules into a feasible overall solution. The objective of their model consists of travel time, nurse busyness, and quality of service, which includes continuity of care.
The authors in [[13]]() combine travel time with continuity of care and preference costs between nurses and clients.

When it comes to the nurse assignment, continuity of care is often studied together with the workload balance of the nurses. In many of these studies, the main focus is to optimize the workload balance, while continuity of care is of secondary importance by including it as a constraint (e.g., by limiting the number of unique nurses that visit a client), see for example [[32]]() , [[33]]() , [[34]]() .

In terms of rostering, our approach parallels that of [[38]]() , where we primarily focus on continuity of care rather than an even distribution of workload among nurses. Albeit we do not specifically balance the workload, our shift design and penalty for overtime implicitly allow for a balanced workload distribution.

To solve the set partitioning problem, the authors employ a large neighborhood search method to generate potential routes, which are then assigned to nurses using a constructive set partitioning heuristic.

In [[20]]() the HHCRSP is solved using a branch-and-price algorithm. This algorithm is divided into two main components: a pricing problem that generates daily schedules for individual nurses and a master problem that combines these daily schedules into a feasible overall solution.
The optimization of the dynamic method is carried out with paGOMEA on a day-by-day basis, using (12) as the fitness function.

In a home care setting, high-quality care is typically associated with continuity of care. In addition, the increasing pressure due to labor shortages calls for cost-efficient operations. This paper focuses on obtaining cost-efficient daily schedules over a longer time horizon, with balanced shift lengths, while ensuring continuity of care (using the continuity of care index). To address this challenge, we propose a novel method based on blueprint routes. This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages. To ensure continuity of care, the daily scheduling decisions are strategically guided using the concept named blueprint routes. The blueprint routes are pre-optimized (partial) routes that help to align the daily schedules to achieve continuity of care in the subsequent nurse-to-shift assignment. Model-based evolutionary algorithms are employed to overcome the NP-hardness of the routing problem and nurse-to-shift assignment. Real-life-based numerical experiments demonstrate that continuity of care does not have to compromise home care schedule costs significantly.

assesses continuity of care in relation to home care scheduling, using the Continuity of Care Index (CCI), as defined in, e.g., [[7]]() , [[14]]() and used in, e.g., [[11]]() . The CCI is designed to mitigate potential issues associated with other measurement methods. It incorporates factors such as the frequency of visits, the range of nurses involved, and the care coordination among nurses

The blueprint routes, that are pre-optimized upfront, comprise sets of (partial) efficient routes via jobs with frequent occurrences throughout the time horizon. These blueprint routes are designed to serve as guiding structures during the daily scheduling optimization, laying the foundation to facilitate continuity of care in the nurse assignment over the complete time horizon.

excerpts: Laps Care , a system that was developed in 2002, uses operations research modeling to eliminate the manual planning of home care unit assignments. More than 200 units/organizations in Swedish municipalities use Laps Care each day to plan staff scheduling and routing for 4,000 home care workers. The system has increased operational efficiency by 10–15 percent; this corresponds to an annual savings of 20–30 million euros ($30–$45 million).

The City of Stockholm, with its 800,000 inhabitants, adopted Laps Care in 2006 and started a full implementation and rollout during 2008, thus adding 800 units and 15,000 home care workers to the system. The savings for the City of Stockholm will be 20–30 million euros ($30–$45 million).

excerpts: Grenouilleau et al. ( 2019 ) formulated the HHCRSP as a set partitioning problem that took into account time windows, skill sets, CoC, travel time, and workload.

Nickel et al. ( 2012 ) proposed a fairly comprehensive HHC planning model that considered nurses’ availability, working hours, qualifications patient time windows and visit frequency. The model was solved with a two-stage approach where a feasible solution is provided by a constraint programming heuristic in the first stage and improved by adaptive large neighbourhood search in the second stage. A model to construct a good master schedule that centred on a high degree of continuity was also proposed. Real-world data for up to 7 days, 12 nurses and 95 visits were solved within 2 hours.

The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. In this article, we investigate a multi-day multi-shift home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem. Decisions made for each period consequently impact decisions for subsequent periods. Each patient requires a given number of visits per day, with specific requirements for each visit. All visits must be conducted while adhering to their requirements as hard constraints. Additionally, each caregiver has a certain qualification level and is pre-scheduled to work specific shifts during the planning horizon.

We address three objectives: minimizing the number of extra shifts, maximizing the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimizing the sum of route durations. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of constraints, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts: Laps Care , a system that was developed in 2002, uses operations research modeling to eliminate the manual planning of home care unit assignments. More than 200 units/organizations in Swedish municipalities use Laps Care each day to plan staff scheduling and routing for 4,000 home care workers. The system has increased operational efficiency by 10–15 percent; this corresponds to an annual savings of 20–30 million euros ($30–$45 million).

The City of Stockholm, with its 800,000 inhabitants, adopted Laps Care in 2006 and started a full implementation and rollout during 2008, thus adding 800 units and 15,000 home care workers to the system. The savings for the City of Stockholm will be 20–30 million euros ($30–$45 million).

excerpts: Effective planning of human resources is critical in designing an efficient home healthcare system. In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts: In terms of rostering, our approach parallels that of [[38]]() , where we primarily focus on continuity of care rather than an even distribution of workload among nurses. Albeit we do not specifically balance the workload, our shift design and penalty for overtime implicitly allow for a balanced workload distribution.

To solve the set partitioning problem, the authors employ a large neighborhood search method to generate potential routes, which are then assigned to nurses using a constructive set partitioning heuristic.

In [[20]]() the HHCRSP is solved using a branch-and-price algorithm. This algorithm is divided into two main components: a pricing problem that generates daily schedules for individual nurses and a master problem that combines these daily schedules into a feasible overall solution.

excerpts: Grenouilleau et al. ( 2019 ) formulated the HHCRSP as a set partitioning problem that took into account time windows, skill sets, CoC, travel time, and workload.

Nickel et al. ( 2012 ) proposed a fairly comprehensive HHC planning model that considered nurses’ availability, working hours, qualifications patient time windows and visit frequency. The model was solved with a two-stage approach where a feasible solution is provided by a constraint programming heuristic in the first stage and improved by adaptive large neighbourhood search in the second stage.

Nickel et al. ( 2012 ) proposed a fairly comprehensive HHC planning model that considered nurses’ availability, working hours, qualifications patient time windows and visit frequency. The model was solved with a two-stage approach where a feasible solution is provided by a constraint programming heuristic in the first stage and improved by adaptive large neighbourhood search in the second stage. A model to construct a good master schedule that centred on a high degree of continuity was also proposed. Real-world data for up to 7 days, 12 nurses and 95 visits were solved within 2 hours.

excerpts: Effective planning of human resources is critical in designing an efficient home healthcare system. In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts: In a home care setting, high-quality care is typically associated with continuity of care. In addition, the increasing pressure due to labor shortages calls for cost-efficient operations. This paper focuses on obtaining cost-efficient daily schedules over a longer time horizon, with balanced shift lengths, while ensuring continuity of care (using the continuity of care index). To address this challenge, we propose a novel method based on blueprint routes. This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages. To ensure continuity of care, the daily scheduling decisions are strategically guided using the concept named blueprint routes. The blueprint routes are pre-optimized (partial) routes that help to align the daily schedules to achieve continuity of care in the subsequent nurse-to-shift assignment. Model-based evolutionary algorithms are employed to overcome the NP-hardness of the routing problem and nurse-to-shift assignment. Real-life-based numerical experiments demonstrate that continuity of care does not have to compromise home care schedule costs significantly.

The blueprint routes, that are pre-optimized upfront, comprise sets of (partial) efficient routes via jobs with frequent occurrences throughout the time horizon. These blueprint routes are designed to serve as guiding structures during the daily scheduling optimization, laying the foundation to facilitate continuity of care in the nurse assignment over the complete time horizon.
The costs associated to the routes include travel time, continuity of care scores, shift length violations, and the number of unmet client preferences.

excerpts: The HHCRSP model is built to minimize the total operating cost and maximize the work balance of the caregiver, which increases the complexity of the problem.

To sum up, most HHCRSP studies usually consider the goal of minimizing cost or distance, ignoring the workload balance and skill matching problems in the actual service process, which results in a large deviation from the actual situation.
The existing research distinguishes the home health care service and home delivery service by using the skill level of the caregiver, which simplifies the combination of problems to a certain extent.

Therefore, this paper introduces the home delivery route separately, and considers the skills matching problem and the home delivery problem on the basis of the traditional home health care scheduling problem.

excerpts: BD** : Logic-Based Benders Decomposition

Effective planning of human resources is critical in designing an efficient home healthcare system. In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts: In a home care setting, high-quality care is typically associated with continuity of care. In addition, the increasing pressure due to labor shortages calls for cost-efficient operations. This paper focuses on obtaining cost-efficient daily schedules over a longer time horizon, with balanced shift lengths, while ensuring continuity of care (using the continuity of care index). To address this challenge, we propose a novel method based on blueprint routes. This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages. To ensure continuity of care, the daily scheduling decisions are strategically guided using the concept named blueprint routes. The blueprint routes are pre-optimized (partial) routes that help to align the daily schedules to achieve continuity of care in the subsequent nurse-to-shift assignment. Model-based evolutionary algorithms are employed to overcome the NP-hardness of the routing problem and nurse-to-shift assignment. Real-life-based numerical experiments demonstrate that continuity of care does not have to compromise home care schedule costs significantly.

The blueprint routes, that are pre-optimized upfront, comprise sets of (partial) efficient routes via jobs with frequent occurrences throughout the time horizon. These blueprint routes are designed to serve as guiding structures during the daily scheduling optimization, laying the foundation to facilitate continuity of care in the nurse assignment over the complete time horizon.
This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages.

excerpts: BD** : Logic-Based Benders Decomposition

Effective planning of human resources is critical in designing an efficient home healthcare system. In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts: Nickel et al. ( 2012 ) proposed a fairly comprehensive HHC planning model that considered nurses’ availability, working hours, qualifications patient time windows and visit frequency. The model was solved with a two-stage approach where a feasible solution is provided by a constraint programming heuristic in the first stage and improved by adaptive large neighbourhood search in the second stage. A model to construct a good master schedule that centred on a high degree of continuity was also proposed. Real-world data for up to 7 days, 12 nurses and 95 visits were solved within 2 hours.

Nickel et al. ( 2012 ) proposed a fairly comprehensive HHC planning model that considered nurses’ availability, working hours, qualifications patient time windows and visit frequency. The model was solved with a two-stage approach where a feasible solution is provided by a constraint programming heuristic in the first stage and improved by adaptive large neighbourhood search in the second stage.
Solutions were found with an open source code called OptaPlanner, which relies on metaheuristics.

excerpts: In [[20]]() the HHCRSP is solved using a branch-and-price algorithm. This algorithm is divided into two main components: a pricing problem that generates daily schedules for individual nurses and a master problem that combines these daily schedules into a feasible overall solution.

To solve the set partitioning problem, the authors employ a large neighborhood search method to generate potential routes, which are then assigned to nurses using a constructive set partitioning heuristic.

In terms of rostering, our approach parallels that of [[38]]() , where we primarily focus on continuity of care rather than an even distribution of workload among nurses. Albeit we do not specifically balance the workload, our shift design and penalty for overtime implicitly allow for a balanced workload distribution.

In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts: Nickel et al. ( 2012 ) proposed a fairly comprehensive HHC planning model that considered nurses’ availability, working hours, qualifications patient time windows and visit frequency. The model was solved with a two-stage approach where a feasible solution is provided by a constraint programming heuristic in the first stage and improved by adaptive large neighbourhood search in the second stage. A model to construct a good master schedule that centred on a high degree of continuity was also proposed. Real-world data for up to 7 days, 12 nurses and 95 visits were solved within 2 hours.
Solutions were found with an open source code called OptaPlanner, which relies on metaheuristics.

basis.reasoning: The field value describes a precise mathematical programming formulation for HHCRSP that uses a master problem to select an optimal set of feasible single-caregiver routes, represented as columns, ensuring each patient visit is covered exactly once. It also states the objective is a weighted sum of costs (travel time, overtime, idle time) and penalties for soft constraint violations (like patient preferences or continuity), with routes satisfying hard constraints such as time windows and required skills. Excerpts that discuss solving HHCRSP via a branch-and-price approach, where a master problem assembles daily schedules (columns) into a feasible whole, directly support the idea of a column-based master problem controlling coverage and overall cost. Excerpts describing set partitioning as the method to generate and assign routes, and constructive heuristics to allocate routes to nurses, align with the concept of representing routes as columns and selecting among them to cover all visits. Additional excerpts that enumerate the types of constraints used in home healthcare optimization—time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity—and objectives (minimizing route duration, shifts, or total travel) corroborate the presence of hard/soft constraints and cost components central to the described formulation. Together, these pieces map well onto a formal description where a master problem selects a feasible set of routes (columns) to cover all visits exactly once, optimizing a weighted cost function that includes travel, overtime, idle time, and penalties for soft constraint violations, while enforcing time windows and skills.

excerpts: In [[20]]() the HHCRSP is solved using a branch-and-price algorithm. This algorithm is divided into two main components: a pricing problem that generates daily schedules for individual nurses and a master problem that combines these daily schedules into a feasible overall solution.

To solve the set partitioning problem, the authors employ a large neighborhood search method to generate potential routes, which are then assigned to nurses using a constructive set partitioning heuristic.

In terms of rostering, our approach parallels that of [[38]]() , where we primarily focus on continuity of care rather than an even distribution of workload among nurses. Albeit we do not specifically balance the workload, our shift design and penalty for overtime implicitly allow for a balanced workload distribution.
The optimization of the dynamic method is carried out with paGOMEA on a day-by-day basis, using (12) as the fitness function.

excerpts: This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages.

When it comes to the nurse assignment, continuity of care is often studied together with the workload balance of the nurses. In many of these studies, the main focus is to optimize the workload balance, while continuity of care is of secondary importance by including it as a constraint (e.g., by limiting the number of unique nurses that visit a client), see for example [[32]]() , [[33]]() , [[34]]() .
The costs associated to the routes include travel time, continuity of care scores, shift length violations, and the number of unmet client preferences.

excerpts: In a home care setting, high-quality care is typically associated with continuity of care. In addition, the increasing pressure due to labor shortages calls for cost-efficient operations. This paper focuses on obtaining cost-efficient daily schedules over a longer time horizon, with balanced shift lengths, while ensuring continuity of care (using the continuity of care index). To address this challenge, we propose a novel method based on blueprint routes. This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages. To ensure continuity of care, the daily scheduling decisions are strategically guided using the concept named blueprint routes. The blueprint routes are pre-optimized (partial) routes that help to align the daily schedules to achieve continuity of care in the subsequent nurse-to-shift assignment. Model-based evolutionary algorithms are employed to overcome the NP-hardness of the routing problem and nurse-to-shift assignment. Real-life-based numerical experiments demonstrate that continuity of care does not have to compromise home care schedule costs significantly.
This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages.
The costs associated to the routes include travel time, continuity of care scores, shift length violations, and the number of unmet client preferences.

When it comes to the nurse assignment, continuity of care is often studied together with the workload balance of the nurses. In many of these studies, the main focus is to optimize the workload balance, while continuity of care is of secondary importance by including it as a constraint (e.g., by limiting the number of unique nurses that visit a client), see for example [[32]]() , [[33]]() , [[34]]() .
The optimization of the dynamic method is carried out with paGOMEA on a day-by-day basis, using (12) as the fitness function.

excerpts: Effective planning of human resources is critical in designing an efficient home healthcare system. In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

excerpts: The HHCRSP model is built to minimize the total operating cost and maximize the work balance of the caregiver, which increases the complexity of the problem.

To sum up, most HHCRSP studies usually consider the goal of minimizing cost or distance, ignoring the workload balance and skill matching problems in the actual service process, which results in a large deviation from the actual situation.
The existing research distinguishes the home health care service and home delivery service by using the skill level of the caregiver, which simplifies the combination of problems to a certain extent.

Therefore, this paper introduces the home delivery route separately, and considers the skills matching problem and the home delivery problem on the basis of the traditional home health care scheduling problem.

This paper focuses on obtaining cost-efficient daily schedules over a longer time horizon, with balanced shift lengths, while ensuring continuity of care (using the continuity of care index). To address this challenge, we propose a novel method based on blueprint routes. This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages. To ensure continuity of care, the daily scheduling decisions are strategically guided using the concept named blueprint routes. The blueprint routes are pre-optimized (partial) routes that help to align the daily schedules to achieve continuity of care in the subsequent nurse-to-shift assignment. Model-based evolutionary algorithms are employed to overcome the NP-hardness of the routing problem and nurse-to-shift assignment. Real-life-based numerical experiments demonstrate that continuity of care does not have to compromise home care schedule costs significantly.

excerpts: The HHCRSP model is built to minimize the total operating cost and maximize the work balance of the caregiver, which increases the complexity of the problem.

To sum up, most HHCRSP studies usually consider the goal of minimizing cost or distance, ignoring the workload balance and skill matching problems in the actual service process, which results in a large deviation from the actual situation.
The existing research distinguishes the home health care service and home delivery service by using the skill level of the caregiver, which simplifies the combination of problems to a certain extent.

Therefore, this paper introduces the home delivery route separately, and considers the skills matching problem and the home delivery problem on the basis of the traditional home health care scheduling problem.

excerpts: The HHCRSP model is built to minimize the total operating cost and maximize the work balance of the caregiver, which increases the complexity of the problem.

To sum up, most HHCRSP studies usually consider the goal of minimizing cost or distance, ignoring the workload balance and skill matching problems in the actual service process, which results in a large deviation from the actual situation.
The existing research distinguishes the home health care service and home delivery service by using the skill level of the caregiver, which simplifies the combination of problems to a certain extent.

Therefore, this paper introduces the home delivery route separately, and considers the skills matching problem and the home delivery problem on the basis of the traditional home health care scheduling problem.

excerpts: NS** : Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search

We address three objectives: minimizing the number of extra shifts, maximizing the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimizing the sum of route durations. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of constraints, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

Effective planning of human resources is critical in designing an efficient home healthcare system. In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

In this article, we investigate a multi-day multi-shift home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem. Decisions made for each period consequently impact decisions for subsequent periods. Each patient requires a given number of visits per day, with specific requirements for each visit. All visits must be conducted while adhering to their requirements as hard constraints. Additionally, each caregiver has a certain qualification level and is pre-scheduled to work specific shifts during the planning horizon.

excerpts: In a home care setting, high-quality care is typically associated with continuity of care. In addition, the increasing pressure due to labor shortages calls for cost-efficient operations. This paper focuses on obtaining cost-efficient daily schedules over a longer time horizon, with balanced shift lengths, while ensuring continuity of care (using the continuity of care index). To address this challenge, we propose a novel method based on blueprint routes. This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages. To ensure continuity of care, the daily scheduling decisions are strategically guided using the concept named blueprint routes. The blueprint routes are pre-optimized (partial) routes that help to align the daily schedules to achieve continuity of care in the subsequent nurse-to-shift assignment. Model-based evolutionary algorithms are employed to overcome the NP-hardness of the routing problem and nurse-to-shift assignment. Real-life-based numerical experiments demonstrate that continuity of care does not have to compromise home care schedule costs significantly.

The blueprint routes, that are pre-optimized upfront, comprise sets of (partial) efficient routes via jobs with frequent occurrences throughout the time horizon. These blueprint routes are designed to serve as guiding structures during the daily scheduling optimization, laying the foundation to facilitate continuity of care in the nurse assignment over the complete time horizon.

A model to construct a good master schedule that centred on a high degree of continuity was also proposed. Real-world data for up to 7 days, 12 nurses and 95 visits were solved within 2 hours.

We address three objectives: minimizing the number of extra shifts, maximizing the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimizing the sum of route durations. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of constraints, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

Effective planning of human resources is critical in designing an efficient home healthcare system. In this study, we present a novel home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem inspired by a real-world application. The proposed problem addresses a set of patients, with varying daily visit requirements, being served by a set of caregivers with different qualification levels over a multi-day multi-shift planning horizon. The problem aims to minimize the number of extra shifts for caregivers, maximize the allocation of caregivers to emergencies, and minimize the sum of route durations over the planning horizon. These objectives are optimized hierarchically while considering a set of restrictions, including time windows, skill matching, synchronization, care continuity, and labor regulations.

In this article, we investigate a multi-day multi-shift home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling problem. Decisions made for each period consequently impact decisions for subsequent periods. Each patient requires a given number of visits per day, with specific requirements for each visit. All visits must be conducted while adhering to their requirements as hard constraints. Additionally, each caregiver has a certain qualification level and is pre-scheduled to work specific shifts during the planning horizon.

excerpts: In a home care setting, high-quality care is typically associated with continuity of care. In addition, the increasing pressure due to labor shortages calls for cost-efficient operations. This paper focuses on obtaining cost-efficient daily schedules over a longer time horizon, with balanced shift lengths, while ensuring continuity of care (using the continuity of care index). To address this challenge, we propose a novel method based on blueprint routes. This method generates daily schedules by constructing optimized shifts and routes with regard to travel time, (time window) waiting time, and shift costs based on hourly wages. To ensure continuity of care, the daily scheduling decisions are strategically guided using the concept named blueprint routes. The blueprint routes are pre-optimized (partial) routes that help to align the daily schedules to achieve continuity of care in the subsequent nurse-to-shift assignment. Model-based evolutionary algorithms are employed to overcome the NP-hardness of the routing problem and nurse-to-shift assignment. Real-life-based numerical experiments demonstrate that continuity of care does not have to compromise home care schedule costs significantly.

The blueprint routes, that are pre-optimized upfront, comprise sets of (partial) efficient routes via jobs with frequent occurrences throughout the time horizon. These blueprint routes are designed to serve as guiding structures during the daily scheduling optimization, laying the foundation to facilitate continuity of care in the nurse assignment over the complete time horizon.

The most directly supportive evidence comes from mentions of Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search, which explicitly identifies ALNS as a core technique used in home healthcare staffing, routing, and scheduling research. Additional direct support comes from explicit references to exact optimization methods such as Mixed Integer Linear Programming, Logic-Based Benders Decomposition, Column Generation, Branch and Price, and related exact/near-exact approaches, which demonstrate the presence of exact methods and structured decomposition techniques in the same research space. The excerpts also collectively emphasize multi-objective and hierarchical optimization with constraints like time windows and skill matching, which align with the overarching goal of robust, feasible solutions in a real-world HHCRSP setting, and they discuss continuity of care as a central objective or constraint, which reinforces the relevance of a holistic, high-quality solution approach. There are also discussions of continuity-focused methodologies (blueprint routes) and multi-day, multi-shift planning that further illustrate how rigorous optimization methods are applied in practice to maintain continuity of care across planning horizons. While constraint programming per se is not explicitly named in the strongest statements, several excerpts document a landscape where exact methods and advanced metaheuristics are used in conjunction with constraint-like structures (time windows, qualifications, synchronization, power of large neighborhoods) to tackle HHCRSPs, which is conceptually consistent with a CP-inspired hybrid framework. Taken together, these excerpts support the notion of a hybrid approach that leverages ALNS, exact methods, and CP-like constraint handling to achieve robust solutions for HHCRSP with continuity considerations.

The next most relevant entries describe ALNS-based approaches to VRP with time windows and synchronized visits, and other hybrid/metaheuristic strategies that incorporate time windows, synchronization, and robustness—these map to the two-stage CP/ALNS paradigm and the ALNS-based improvement concept, as well as the synchronization aspect mentioned in the summary. Subsequent excerpts cover synchronized visits, interdependent services, and nurse-patient continuity constraints, which reinforce the themes of coordinated care and continuous staffing found in the review. Additional sources discuss robustness to uncertain travel/service times and crew scheduling, which support the branch-and-price/cut or rolling-horizon robustness themes described in the field value. Finally, bibliographic surveys and related works provide broader context that corroborates the existence and evolution of these solution methods within HHCRSP literature.

citations.title: Routing and scheduling in Home Health Care: A Literature Survey and Bibliometric Analysis

executive_summary.description: A high-level overview of the challenges and solutions in home care route optimization, summarizing the state-of-the-art in balancing efficiency, staff continuity, and complex operational requirements through mathematical optimization.

problem_formulation.description: Describes the academic definition of the Home Health Care Routing and Scheduling Problem (HHCRSP), a complex variant of the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP), outlining its core structure involving caregivers, patients, visits, and depots.

optimization_objectives.description: Details the multi-objective nature of home care optimization, including common goals such as minimizing travel costs and overtime, maximizing continuity of care, ensuring fair workload distribution among staff, and maximizing patient satisfaction.

constraint_name.description: The name of the operational constraint (e.g., Time Windows, Skill Matching).

description.description: A detailed description of the constraint and its importance.

type.description: The category of the constraint (e.g., Temporal, Assignment, Routing, Legal).

continuity_of_care_models.description: A detailed analysis of how staff continuity (a caregiver consistently visiting the same patient) is modeled and optimized. Includes descriptions of different metrics (e.g., number of unique caregivers, Continuity of Care Index - CCI) and modeling techniques to promote consistency over a planning horizon.

synchronized_and_paired_visits.description: Explains the modeling of double-staff visits where two or more caregivers must be present at a patient's location simultaneously. This includes details on temporal synchronization constraints and pairing requirements.

workload_balancing_techniques.description: Focuses on strategies and mathematical formulations used to ensure a fair and equitable distribution of workload among caregivers. This covers objectives like minimizing the variance in working hours or travel time.

model_name.description: The name of the mathematical programming model (e.g., MILP, Set Partitioning).

description.description: A brief description of how the model is formulated for the HHCRSP.

typical_application.description: Common use cases or problem variants where this model is applied.

mathematical_programming_models.description: A list of common mathematical programming formulations used to model the HHCRSP. For each model, such as Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) and Set Partitioning, provide a brief description of its structure and application.

algorithm_name.description: The name of the exact algorithm (e.g., Branch-and-Price, Column Generation).

description.description: A description of the algorithm's methodology.

role_in_hhcrsp.description: The specific role of the algorithm in solving the HHCRSP (e.g., generating routes in a pricing subproblem).

exact_algorithms.description: A list of exact algorithms used to find optimal solutions for HHCRSP, often for smaller or simplified instances. For each algorithm, such as Branch-and-Price, Column Generation, and Logic-Based Benders Decomposition, describe its role in solving the problem.

algorithm_name.description: The name of the heuristic or metaheuristic algorithm (e.g., ALNS, VNS).

description.description: A brief explanation of how the algorithm works.

approach_type.description: The category of the algorithm (e.g., Local Search, Population-based, Hybrid).

heuristic_and_metaheuristic_algorithms.description: A list of heuristic and metaheuristic methods used to find high-quality solutions for large-scale, real-world HHCRSP instances. For each method, such as Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search (ALNS), Variable Neighborhood Search (VNS), Tabu Search, and Genetic Algorithms, explain its general approach.

constraint_programming_approaches.description: Details the application of Constraint Programming (CP) to the HHCRSP. This includes how CP models handle complex constraints like temporal dependencies and preferences, and mentions the use of solvers like Google OR-Tools' CP-SAT.

stochastic_and_dynamic_optimization.description: Discusses advanced models that account for real-world uncertainty. This includes stochastic programming for variable travel/service times and dynamic models that handle new visit requests or caregiver absences in real-time.

case_study_name.description: The name of the case study or system (e.g., LAPS CARE).

organization.description: The company, vendor, or municipality involved in the case study.

methods_used.description: The optimization methods and algorithms implemented.

reported_outcomes.description: The documented improvements in efficiency, cost savings, or quality of care.

industry_case_studies_and_best_practices.description: A collection of real-world applications and industry best practices. For each case study, such as LAPS CARE in Sweden or collaborations with vendors like AlayaCare, summarize the implementation, methods used, and reported outcomes in efficiency and quality of care.

tool_name.description: The name of the software, library, or solver (e.g., Google OR-Tools).

developer.description: The company or organization that develops the tool.

type.description: The type of tool (e.g., CP Solver, Open-source library, Commercial software).

relevance_to_hhcrsp.description: How the tool is specifically used for solving home care scheduling problems.

optimization_software_and_solvers.description: A list of software tools and libraries used in both academia and industry for home care optimization. For each tool, such as OptaPlanner, Google OR-Tools, and IBM CP Optimizer, provide a brief description of its capabilities and relevance to HHCRSP.

dataset_name.description: The commonly used name for the benchmark dataset.

originator.description: The author(s) or institution that created the dataset (e.g., Bredström and Rönnqvist).

description.description: A brief description of the dataset, including its size and characteristics.

title.description: The title of the academic paper.

summary_of_scope.description: A brief summary of the topics and period covered by the review.

key_literature_reviews_and_surveys.description: A curated list of important academic survey and review papers that provide a comprehensive overview of the HHCRSP literature. For each paper, provide the title, authors, and a brief summary of its scope.

summary_of_research_trends.description: A concluding summary of current and future research directions in home care optimization, such as the integration of machine learning for travel time prediction, robust optimization, and more sophisticated models for patient and caregiver preferences.
